response:
# Breadcrumb Navigation Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users, keyboard-only users, low vision users, screen magnifier users > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, VoiceOver, keyboard-only navigation **Feature:** A reusable e-commerce breadcrumb navigation component showing the current page hierarchy, such as `Home > Products > Electronics > Laptops`. **Risk Level:** Medium **Component/Page Type:** Breadcrumb navigation / site wayfinding component --- ## Scope & Context This component provides page hierarchy navigation for an e-commerce website. It helps users understand where the current page sits in the site structure and move back to parent categories. The breadcrumb depth varies from 2 to 5 levels. Every breadcrumb item except the final current page item is a link. The final item is static text and must be identified programmatically as the current page. This plan is not claiming to solve global site navigation, search navigation, category menu behavior, or product filtering. It covers only the breadcrumb component and its direct accessibility requirements. Primary accessibility risks: - Breadcrumbs could be announced as disconnected links instead of a structured navigation path. - The current page could be visually indicated but not programmatically exposed. - Separator characters such as `>` could be announced by screen readers and add noise. - Link text could be over-labeled, creating repetitive announcements such as “Go to Products link.” - Focus indicators could be insufficient or obscured in dense page layouts. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan Use a semantic `nav` landmark with an accessible label that identifies it as breadcrumb navigation. Use an ordered list because breadcrumbs represent a sequence from broadest ancestor to current page. Each breadcrumb item should be an `li`. The current page item should remain static text, not a link. This prevents keyboard users from tabbing to a link that navigates to the page they are already on. ### Structure Diagram ```html
  1. Home
  2. Products
  3. Electronics
  4. Laptops
``` ### Semantic Decisions | Element | Purpose | Accessibility Reason | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---| | `nav` | Defines breadcrumb as a navigation region | Allows screen reader users to discover the breadcrumb via landmarks | WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks | | `aria-label="Breadcrumb"` | Names the navigation landmark | Distinguishes breadcrumb nav from primary/footer navigation | WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels, 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value | | `ol` | Represents ordered page hierarchy | Communicates sequence and number of levels | WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships | | `li` | Represents each breadcrumb item | Preserves list semantics for assistive tech | WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships | | `a` for ancestors | Navigates to parent pages | Native link semantics and keyboard behavior | WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard, 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value | | Static current item | Identifies current page without redundant navigation | Avoids unnecessary self-link in tab order | WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order, 2.4.4 Link Purpose | | `aria-current="page"` | Programmatically marks current page | Announces current location to screen reader users | WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value | --- ## Interaction Pattern Design Breadcrumbs are primarily native navigation links, not a custom ARIA widget. There is no need for a menu, disclosure, toolbar, or roving tabindex pattern. ### APG Mapping There is no dedicated WAI-ARIA APG breadcrumb interaction pattern requiring custom keyboard behavior. The correct pattern is semantic navigation using native links inside a labeled navigation landmark. Reference: - WAI-ARIA APG Landmark Regions: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/landmark-regions/ - WAI-ARIA `aria-current`: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/names-and-descriptions/#currentitem ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG / Semantic Pattern | Keyboard Model | ARIA Attributes | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Breadcrumb navigation region | Landmark navigation region | Landmark discoverable by screen reader shortcuts | `aria-label="Breadcrumb"` | 1.3.1, 2.4.1, 2.4.6, 4.1.2 | | Ancestor breadcrumb links | Native link pattern | `Tab` moves to each link; `Enter` activates link | None required beyond accessible link text | 2.1.1, 2.4.3, 2.4.4, 4.1.2 | | Current page item | Current item indicator | Not focusable; read as current page in list sequence | `aria-current="page"` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Visual separators | Decorative presentation only | Not focusable; not announced | Prefer CSS pseudo-elements or hidden decorative spans | 1.1.1, 1.3.1 | ### Keyboard Model - `Tab`: moves focus through each linked breadcrumb item from left to right. - `Enter`: activates the focused breadcrumb link and navigates to that parent page. - `Space`: no special handling; native links do not require Space activation. - Arrow keys: no custom behavior. Breadcrumb links are not a composite widget. - Escape: no behavior. There is no overlay or dismissible state. Expected tab order for `Home > Products > Electronics > Laptops`: 1. `Home` link 2. `Products` link 3. `Electronics` link 4. Next focusable element after the breadcrumb `Laptops` is not included in the tab order because it is the current page text, not a link. --- ## Link Text Strategy Use concise page/category names as the visible link text: - Good: `Home`, `Products`, `Electronics` - Avoid: `Go to Home`, `Go to Products`, `Go to Electronics` The context is already provided by the breadcrumb navigation landmark and ordered list. Adding “Go to” makes screen reader output more verbose without improving link purpose. The current page text should match the page or category title when possible: - Good: `Laptops` - Avoid: `Current page: Laptops` as visible text The current state should be exposed through `aria-current="page"`, not baked into the visible label unless the design specifically requires visible current-page wording. If a breadcrumb label is ambiguous outside context, prefer the real category name over generic text. Do not use “Back,” “Previous,” “View,” “More,” or “Click here.” WCAG citations: - WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose: link destination must be clear from link text or context. - WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels: labels should describe topic or purpose. - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value: link accessible names must be available programmatically. --- ## Focus Management Plan No focus trap, focus restoration, roving tabindex, or dynamic focus movement is required. Focus behavior should remain native and predictable: 1. The breadcrumb `nav` itself is not focusable. 2. Each ancestor link is focusable in DOM order. 3. The current page item is static text and not focusable. 4. Focus proceeds to the next interactive element after the breadcrumb. 5. DOM order must match visual order: `Home`, `Products`, `Electronics`, `Laptops`. Focus indicators: - Use the browser default focus indicator or a custom `:focus-visible` style. - Custom focus indicators must meet at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. - The focus outline must not be clipped by overflow containers. - If the breadcrumb sits under a sticky header, focused breadcrumb links must remain at least partially visible. WCAG citations: - WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard: all breadcrumb links must be keyboard operable. - WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order: tab order must match page hierarchy order. - WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible: focused links must have visible focus indication. - WCAG 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured: focused breadcrumb links must not be hidden by sticky UI. - WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance: custom focus indicators should have sufficient area and contrast. --- ## State Communication Design ### State Communication Table | State | Visual Indicator | Programmatic Indicator | ARIA Attribute | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Ancestor item is navigable | Link styling, underline or clear link treatment | Native anchor exposes link role and destination | None required | 2.4.4, 4.1.2 | | Current page | Final item may use current text styling, stronger weight, or muted color | Current page exposed to assistive tech | `aria-current="page"` | 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | | Focused link | Visible outline/ring | Browser focus state | Native focus; no ARIA needed | 2.4.7, 2.4.13 | | Separator between items | Visual arrow, slash, or chevron | Decorative; not included in accessible name or reading flow | None if CSS-only; `aria-hidden="true"` if markup is used | 1.1.1, 1.3.1 | | Disabled state | Not applicable | Breadcrumb links should not be disabled | Do not use `aria-disabled` | 4.1.2 | | Expanded/collapsed | Not applicable | Breadcrumb has no disclosure behavior | Do not use `aria-expanded` | 4.1.2 | | Selected/unselected | Not applicable | Current page is not a selectable tab/listbox item | Do not use `aria-selected` | 4.1.2 | | Loading/busy | Not in base component | If breadcrumbs are client-loaded, wrap status outside component | `aria-busy` only if dynamic loading exists | 4.1.3 | The weakest point in many breadcrumb implementations is separator handling. If separators are literal text nodes, screen readers may announce “greater than” between every item. That is noise, not useful structure. Use CSS-generated separators when possible. If actual markup is required, wrap the separator in an element with `aria-hidden="true"` and ensure it is not focusable. --- ## Visual Accessibility Plan The breadcrumb should remain readable, distinguishable, and usable across visual conditions. Requirements: - Link text must meet 4.5:1 contrast against the background for normal text. - Current page text must also meet 4.5:1 contrast; do not make it too faint. - Link distinction in prose-like breadcrumb text should not rely on color alone. Use underline, font weight, or another visual distinction. - Focus indicators must meet 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. - Targets should be at least 24x24 CSS pixels minimum, with 44x44 preferred when spacing allows. - At 200% zoom, breadcrumbs must wrap or collapse without horizontal scrolling. - At 400% zoom / 320px equivalent width, all breadcrumb information must remain available. Do not hide parent links entirely unless an accessible alternative is provided. - Separators must not be the only indicator of hierarchy; the ordered list structure provides programmatic hierarchy. WCAG citations: - WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color: links/current state must not rely only on color. - WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum: text contrast must meet AA. - WCAG 1.4.4 Resize Text: text must resize to 200%. - WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow: no two-dimensional scrolling at narrow widths. - WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast: focus indicators and UI boundaries require sufficient contrast. - WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum: pointer targets should be at least 24x24 CSS pixels. --- ## Content Accessibility Plan Breadcrumb labels should be short, stable, and reflect actual page or category names. For the example path: - `Home` - `Products` - `Electronics` - `Laptops` Do not add visually hidden prefixes like “Go to” unless there is a proven ambiguity. In this component, the `nav aria-label="Breadcrumb"` context is sufficient. Separator strategy: - Preferred: CSS-only separators using borders, background images, or pseudo-elements that are not exposed as meaningful content. - Acceptable fallback: separator markup with `aria-hidden="true"`. - Avoid: literal `>` text nodes between list items. Reading order: - DOM order must match visual order. - Do not use CSS flex reversal or visual reordering. - For right-to-left languages, plan separately; this plan assumes English left-to-right content. Language: - The page root should define the document language, such as `lang="en"`. - If breadcrumb labels include a different language, mark the language change on that label. WCAG citations: - WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content: decorative separators must be hidden from assistive tech. - WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships: list and current-page relationships must be programmatic. - WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence: DOM and visual order must match. - WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose: link labels must identify destination. - WCAG 3.1.1 Language of Page and 3.1.2 Language of Parts: language must be programmatically identifiable. --- ## Testing Strategy ### Automated Testing Run axe-core on pages containing breadcrumb variants: - 2-level breadcrumb - 3-level breadcrumb - 5-level breadcrumb - Long breadcrumb labels - Current page label only at final item Verify: - Navigation landmark has an accessible name. - Links have accessible names. - No invalid ARIA attributes. - `aria-current="page"` is used only for the current item. - Contrast passes for links, current item, and focus styles. ### DOM Verification Inspect rendered DOM, not just component snapshots: - `nav` exists and has `aria-label="Breadcrumb"`. - Breadcrumb items are inside an `ol`. - Each breadcrumb entry is inside an `li`. - Ancestor items contain links. - Current item is not an anchor. - Current item has `aria-current="page"` on the current item or current item text container. - Separator characters are not present as readable text nodes, or are inside `aria-hidden="true"` elements. - No `title` attributes are used as the accessible name mechanism. ### Keyboard Testing Test with real keyboard navigation: - `Tab` focuses `Home`. - `Tab` focuses `Products`. - `Tab` focuses `Electronics`. - `Tab` skips `Laptops` because it is static current-page text. - `Enter` on each focused breadcrumb link navigates to the correct parent page. - Focus indicator is visible on each focused link. - Focus order matches visual order. ### Screen Reader Testing Test with NVDA and VoiceOver: - Breadcrumb is discoverable as a navigation landmark named “Breadcrumb.” - The ordered list is announced as a list with the expected number of items. - Ancestor items are announced as links with concise names, e.g. “Products, link.” - Current page is announced with current-page state, e.g. “Laptops, current page.” - Separators are not announced as “greater than,” “slash,” “chevron,” or similar visual noise. ### Visual Testing Verify: - Text contrast meets 4.5:1. - Focus indicator meets 3:1 and is not clipped. - Links are visually distinguishable by more than color where needed. - Breadcrumb wraps or otherwise reflows at 200% and 400% zoom. - Touch targets meet at least 24x24 CSS pixels. --- ## Test Cases - Keyboard navigation: Tab through all breadcrumb links and verify logical left-to-right order. - Keyboard activation: Focus each ancestor link, press Enter, verify navigation target. - Current item: Verify final breadcrumb is not focusable and does not navigate. - ARIA current: Verify only the final current page item has `aria-current="page"`. - Landmark: Verify screen reader landmark list includes “Breadcrumb.” - List semantics: Verify screen reader announces breadcrumb as an ordered list. - Separator handling: Verify separators are not announced. - Link text: Verify links are named `Home`, `Products`, and `Electronics`, not verbose labels like `Go to Products`. - Contrast: Verify breadcrumb text and focus indicators meet WCAG AA contrast. - Reflow: Verify 2-5 level breadcrumbs work at 200% zoom and narrow viewport without horizontal scrolling. --- ## Implementation Tasks ### Task 1: Breadcrumb Semantic Structure 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Reusable breadcrumb component file - Component test file - Component styles file **Structure Stub:** ```html
  1. Home
  2. Products
  3. Electronics
  4. Laptops
``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-label="Breadcrumb"` on `nav`: names the navigation landmark. WCAG 2.4.6, 4.1.2. - `aria-current="page"` on the current item: exposes current page state. WCAG 1.3.1, 4.1.2. **Keyboard Interactions:** - `Tab`: move through ancestor links only. - `Enter`: activate focused breadcrumb link. - No custom arrow, Escape, or Space behavior. **Tests:** - Verify `nav[aria-label="Breadcrumb"]` exists. - Verify breadcrumb uses `ol > li`. - Verify all non-current items are links. - Verify final item is not a link. - Verify final item has `aria-current="page"`. **WCAG Criteria:** - WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships - WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard - WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order - WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value --- ### Task 2: Link Text and Current Page Strategy 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Reusable breadcrumb component file - Tests for generated labels and variable depth **Structure Stub:** ```html
  • Products
  • Laptops
    • ``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-current="page"` only on current item. WCAG 4.1.2. - Do not use `aria-label` to add “Go to” unless visible text is ambiguous. WCAG 2.4.4. - Do not use `title` as an accessible name. WCAG 4.1.2. **Keyboard Interactions:** - Same native link model as Task 1. **Tests:** - Verify generated link names match visible category names. - Verify no breadcrumb link relies on `title`. - Verify variable depths from 2 to 5 render correctly. - Verify current page is static text for every supported depth. **WCAG Criteria:** - WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose - WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value --- ### Task 3: Separator and Visual Accessibility Styling 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Component styles file - Visual regression or UI test file **Structure Stub:** ```html
    1. Home
    2. Products
    3. Laptops
    ``` Separators should be produced visually without becoming meaningful screen reader content. **ARIA Attributes:** - No ARIA required if separators are CSS-only. - If separator markup is unavoidable, separator element must use `aria-hidden="true"`. WCAG 1.1.1. **Keyboard Interactions:** - Separators must not be focusable. - Separator styling must not interfere with link focus outlines. **Tests:** - Verify separators are not text nodes announced by screen readers. - Verify no separator is focusable. - Verify focus indicators are visible and not clipped. - Verify breadcrumb reflows at 200% zoom and narrow widths. - Verify text and focus contrast meet WCAG AA. **WCAG Criteria:** - WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content - WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color - WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum - WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow - WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible - WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance - WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum --- ## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints | Checkpoint | After Task | Focus Areas | |---|---|---| | 🔍 1 | Task 1 | Verify semantic `nav`, ordered list structure, current item is static text, and `aria-current="page"` is correctly placed | | 🔍 2 | Task 2 | Verify link text is concise, not over-labeled, and no `title` attributes are used for accessible names | | 🔍 3 | Task 3 | Verify separators are hidden from screen readers, focus indicators are visible, and responsive reflow works | --- ## Architecture Overview The component has one semantic navigation landmark, one ordered list, 2-5 list items, native links for ancestor pages, and one static current page item. It uses native browser keyboard behavior rather than a custom ARIA widget. The only required ARIA attributes are `aria-label="Breadcrumb"` for the landmark and `aria-current="page"` for the current page item. --- ## Failure Modes This plan prevents the following likely defects: - Breadcrumb rendered as plain inline links without navigation/list semantics. - Current page shown visually but not exposed with `aria-current="page"`. - Current page incorrectly rendered as a self-link. - Separator characters announced as “greater than” or “slash.” - Links over-labeled with repetitive accessible names such as “Go to Products.” - Focus indicators clipped or too low contrast. - Breadcrumb order visually reversed or changed independently from DOM order. - Variable-depth breadcrumbs missing current-page handling in one branch. --- ## References - WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/ - WAI-ARIA APG Landmark Regions: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/landmark-regions/ - WCAG 2.2 Quick Reference: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/quickref/ - ARIA `aria-current`: https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-1.2/#aria-current
